heading_path: Examining patients who present with flashes and floaters
paragraph_or_text_span: You should refer a patient presenting with flashes and/or floaters to a colleague if you do not feel competent to manage the patient. You should follow local protocols for management and referral of these patients. A285 This Guidance does not change what you must do under the law.

heading_path: Examining patients who present with flashes and floaters
Principles of examining a patient who presents with flashes and floaters
paragraph_or_text_span: A286 If you are unable to carry out an adequate examination when you examine a patient who presents with flashes and/or floaters, you must refer the patient to a practitioner who is competent to do this. A287 You should ensure that front line or support staff are trained to deal with such a patient who contacts the practice. Patients should be told a diagnosis cannot be reached without an examinat

heading_path: Examining patients who present with flashes and floaters
paragraph_or_text_span: A292 The majority of patients presenting with flashes and/or floaters will not have a retinal detachment. If you do not feel competent to manage a patient presenting with flashes and/or floaters, you should refer them to an appropriate colleague. Emergency referrals include: retinal detachment pigment in the anterior vitreous (tobacco dust) vitreous, retinal or pre-retinal haemorrhage lattice dege

heading_path: Examining patients who present with flashes and floaters
paragraph_or_text_span: A294 Most cases of floaters are due to posterior vitreous detachment (PVD) or vitreous degeneration. You can manage a patient in your practice if you confirm they have a PVD after dilated ocular examination and: vision is unchanged no retinal tear or detachment is present no pigment is present in the anterior vitreous the patient is well informed about what symptoms to expect if the retina does br
